subject: ap-chemistry
theme: Reaction rates are controlled by activation energy, concentration, temperature, and catalysis — understanding kinetics reveals how fast reactions proceed and through what sequence of elementary steps.

core_concepts.title: Rate Laws and Reaction Orders
core_concepts.detail: The rate law $rate = k[\text{A}]^m[\text{B}]^n$ is determined experimentally, not from the balanced equation. The order with respect to each reactant ($m$, $n$) is found by comparing experiments where only that reactant's concentration changes. The overall order is $m + n$. The rate constant $k$ depends on temperature but not concentration.

core_concepts.title: Integrated Rate Laws
core_concepts.detail: Zero order: $[\text{A}]_t = [\text{A}]_0 - kt$; plot [A] vs. $t$ gives a straight line. First order: $\ln[\text{A}]_t = \ln[\text{A}]_0 - kt$; plot $\ln[A]$ vs. $t$ gives a straight line; half-life $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$ is constant. Second order: $1/[\text{A}]_t = 1/[\text{A}]_0 + kt$; plot $1/[A]$ vs. $t$ gives a straight line; half-life increases as concentration decreases.

core_concepts.title: Activation Energy and the Arrhenius Equation
core_concepts.detail: The Arrhenius equation $k = Ae^{-E_a/RT}$ shows that $k$ increases exponentially with temperature and decreases with higher $E_a$. Taking the natural log: $\ln k = \ln A - E_a/RT$. A plot of $\ln k$ vs. $1/T$ gives a line with slope $-E_a/R$. The two-point form allows $E_a$ calculation from two rate constants at two temperatures.

core_concepts.title: Collision Theory
core_concepts.detail: For a successful reaction, colliding molecules need: (1) sufficient kinetic energy ($\geq E_a$) and (2) correct orientation. Temperature increases the fraction of molecules with energy $\geq E_a$ (from the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution), dramatically increasing rate. Higher concentration increases collision frequency.

core_concepts.title: Reaction Mechanisms and Rate-Determining Steps
core_concepts.detail: A mechanism is a sequence of elementary steps. Intermediates are formed in one step and consumed in another; they do not appear in the overall equation. The rate-determining step (slowest) sets the rate law for the overall reaction. If a fast equilibrium precedes the slow step, intermediate concentrations must be substituted using the equilibrium expression.

core_concepts.title: Catalysis
core_concepts.detail: A catalyst provides an alternative pathway with lower activation energy, increasing the rate constant $k$ for both forward and reverse reactions equally. A catalyst does not change $\Delta H$, $\Delta G$, or the equilibrium constant. Homogeneous catalysts are in the same phase as reactants; heterogeneous catalysts (e.g., solid Pt in gas-phase reactions) are in a different phase.

formulas.name: First-order half-life
formulas.description: For a first-order reaction, the half-life is independent of concentration and depends only on the rate constant $k$.

formulas.name: Arrhenius equation (two-point form)
formulas.description: Allows calculation of activation energy from rate constants measured at two temperatures; $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, temperatures in Kelvin.
formulas.example: If $k$ doubles from 300 K to 310 K: $\ln 2 = (E_a/8.314)(1/300 - 1/310)$; solve to find $E_a \approx 53$ kJ/mol.

formulas.name: Rate law
formulas.description: Expresses the reaction rate as a function of the rate constant and reactant concentrations raised to empirically determined orders $m$ and $n$.

exam_tip: To identify reaction order from data, always use the method of initial rates: hold one concentration constant and see how rate changes when the other is varied. If doubling [A] doubles rate → first order; if it quadruples → second order; if it has no effect → zero order. The linear plot test (which graph gives a straight line) is the integrated-rate-law approach for verifying order from concentration-vs-time data.
